State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance Administration                    Executive, Legal              Enforcement              Conduct of Interest
(916) 322-3660                    322-5901            322-6441            322-6444

September 23, 1977

Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.
Dobbs and Nielsen
One Maritime Plaza, Suite 2500
San Francisco, CA 94111

A-77-08-236

Dear Mr. Nielsen:

     After receiving my letter of August 26, 1977,
supporting the Franchise Tax Board's audit finding on unreg-
istered committees, you once again expressed your desire to
bring CREPAC and all local BORPACs into compliance with the
campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act.
Accordingly, you ask two questions. First, you ask whether
there were any possible way for CREPAC and local BORPACs to
file a statement as one committee. In terms of the language
of the Act, in other words, could CREPAC and its local BORPACs
be viewed as a combination of persons or as persons acting
in concert? Second, what action should be taken by CREPAC
and its local BORPACs relative to the amendment of campaign
statements previously filed with local jurisdictions through-
out the state?

     In addition to the information contained in my
letter of August 26, the following facts are material to the
conclusions set forth in this letter. In recent telephone
conversations you informed me that local BORPACs are equiv-
alent to local subcommittees of CREPAC. Thus, the finances
and operations of each local BORPAC must be handled in ac-
cordance with CREPAC bylaws. These bylaws can be changed by
the CREPAC directors without the consent of the local BORPACs.
Moreover, the power to write all BORPAC checks and the tech-
nical authority to disapprove a contribution requested by a
BORPAC is retained by an officer of CREPAC.

     Given the facts set forth above, we think it would
be permissible for CREPAC and its local BORPACs to file
campaign statements in the manner set forth in the FTB audit
report or as a single committee. If, however, you should
choose to file as a single committee, your present practice
of filing campaign statements under the name "CREPAC" while
recipients of your contributions simultaneously disclose the
contributor as a local BORPAC must be discontinued. In

Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.
September 23, 1977
Page Two


order to comply with the provisions of the Political Reform
Act, you must take reasonable steps to assure that your
committee will be identified by the same name on its committee statement and on the campaign statements of any person
contributing to or receiving funds from your committee.
Perhaps the transmittal letter included with each contribution
would be the appropriate vehicle for accomplishing this
task. In addition to listing the name of the committee, it
is permissible to ask contribution recipients to include a
reference to a local BORPAC in their campaign statements.
For example, your transmittal letter might suggest that the
contribution from CREPAC be disclosed in any of the following
ways:

     1. CREPAC

     2. CREPAC and 49 BORPACs

     3. CREPAC (Sacramento BORPAC)

     4. CREPAC through its Sacramento BORPAC

     5. Sacramento BORPAC, a subcommittee of CREPAC

Although it is not presently required, we would ask that
your campaign statement break contributions received by
local BORPAC district and expenditures according to the
local BORPAC that authorized them.

     Second, with respect to bringing your previous
statements into compliance, it will be sufficient to file
with each filing officer in possession of a CREPAC statement,
a statement directing persons interested in the BORPAC statement to the CREPAC report. You should direct the filing
officer to file this statement under the name of the local
BORPAC. No additional statements or amendments to the CREPAC
statements need be filed. This advice assumes that all
information relative to contributions and expenditures for
CREPAC and its local BORPACs have been previously reported
on the CREPAC statements and filed with the appropriate
filing officers.

     Pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) and
Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329, good faith compliance with the provisions of this letter will immunize you
from prosecution by the Fair Political Practices Commission
for failure to file campaign statements under the proper
committee organization. You should be advised, however,

Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.
September 23, 1977
Page Three


that this immunity does not extend to an enforcement action
brought by any other person with civil or criminal enforce-
ment responsibility under the Act. If you have further
questions please feel free to contact me.

Sincerely,


[illegible signature]
Ted Prim
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

TP:plh

DOBBS & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law

SUITE 2500, THE ALCOA BUILDING
ONE MARITIME PLAZA
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94111

TELEPHONE (415) 362-1940

September 6, 1977


John A. Gordnier, Esq.
Deputy Attorney General
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 500
Sacramento, California  95814

Re:    PTB Audit of California Real Estate
       Political Action Committee

Dear John:

     Enclosed please find copies of correspondence between
the Franchise Tax Board staff and the Fair Political
Practices Commission staff and myself discussing the FTB's
audit conclusions.

     Since we last talked, the FPPC staff has replied on
August 26, 1977 that in its opinion the local "BORPACs"
are, in fact, unregistered committees. The FTB's finding
was apparently based on the fact that "funds transmitted
by the BORPACs are first applied toward their individual
pre-set goals, the excess funds, if any, earmarked and held
in trust for the local BORPAC" (emphasis added). But as
set forth in my letter of July 21, 1977, it is untrue that
BORPACs have any involvement in the receipt of funds. The
California Association of Realtors invoices each member on
an annual basis requesting payment of national, state and
local dues as well as a voluntary contribution of $10 to
"California Real Estate Political Action Committee." The
local Board of Realtors, not the local BORPAC, receives these
payments, withdraws its local dues, and transmits the entire
remaining amount to the California Association of Realtors in
Los Angeles. CAR then remits to the National Association of
Realtors its portion and remits to CREPAC its portion, if any.
Once the funds are in the CREPAC account, then the accounting
takes place. Only if a group of Realtors has voluntarily
created a "BORPAC", does the statewide board of trustees of
CREPAC consider any of the funds received from Realtors within
that same geographical area to be subject to any control other
than their own. Once a BORPAC is formed, then the designation
of "excess" funds from that geographical area is made to a


SACRAMENTO OFFICE · SUITE 900, 1006 FOURTH STREET, SACRAMENTO CA 95814

John A. Gordnier, Esq.
September 6, 1977
Page two


ledger account maintained by CREPAC in Los Angeles from
which all checks are then written on behalf of the local
BORPAC and sent directly to the candidate or to the BORPAC
representative for his or her delivery.

All contributions made in the name of CREPAC and
BORPAC are reported on CREPAC's report which is also sent
to all counties and cities where local contributions have
been made. When I was retained as legal counsel in 1976,
I recommended that the CREPAC registration be amended to
clearly show that the committee was making contributions
under more than one name, and I further recommended that
the consolidated campaign statement label each contribution
to show in which name it was made. I have been told that
when the staff changed, these recommendations were delayed,
but have now been implemented.

Apparently, the FPPC staff believes that the posting
to a ledger account is, in itself, "a receipt" and believes
that, therefore, each BORPAC whose account has postings of
$500 or more is a committee. After receiving Red Prim's letter,
attached, I called him and found him unable to explain to me why
compliance would not have been sufficient if each of the BORPACs
had registered, or filed a consolidated registration, and then
filed a single consolidated report, pursuant to Government Code
Section 84213(b) and FPPC opinions to Thomas G. Lumsdon (75-205)
and to Harry H. Kahn (75-185). Mr. Prim said he would let me
know, but has not yet responded.

I also asked Mr. Prim whether CREPAC would need to amend
all its reports since January 1975 to delete all contributions
made on behalf of all BORPACs (or only those over $500), and
register and file 1975-1977 reports for each BORPAC. Obviously,
this would not provide any additional information at the office
of any local filing officer (and if under $500 BORPACs are not
required to file, would provide less information); since the
amendments would only separate into two reports what is presently
on file as one report.

Neither could Mr. Prim explain how to handle in the future,
contributions by those local BORPACs who do not exceed $500 in a
calendar year. Under the FPPC ruling, no disclosure would be
required for contributions by CREPAC on behalf of any BORPAC that
had not had $500 posted to its account.

John A. Gordnier, Esq.
September 6, 1977
Page three


     As you can see from this correspondence, and I
confirmed it with Mr. Prim, we are willing to follow what-
ever rules apply, once we are sure what they are.

     Some of the documents mentioned above and enclosed also
relate to the "99 Club." This fundraising organization only
raises monies for federal campaigns. As explained earlier,
the maximum contribution requested for CREPAC on the consoli-
dated dues statement is $10. Separate and apart, individual
Realtors solicit for $99 personal contributions. Such funds
are transmitted by CAR to the national political action committee
(RPAC) which only supports federal candidates. CAR acts as an
agent under the federal rules, and it was always my understanding
that the funds had been deposited into a separate fund pursuant
to federal regulation 102.6 (Federal Register, Vol. 41, No. 166,
August 25, 1976 at 35939). As mentioned in the FPB audit, a
separate 99 Club bank account had been established, but for some
unexplained reason, the former staff, apparently through inadver-
tence, deposited 99 Club monies in the CREPAC account and then
later separated it out and forwarded it to RPAC. All such funds
were reported as the income received by and disbursements made by
CREPAC, since CREPAC knew it had to balance its checkbook with
its state campaign statements.

     If you need any further clarification about the 99 Club,
we can provide correspondence with RPAC, declarations from
California RPAC trustees, or from the CAR staff who serve as
RPAC agents in California. Incidentally, for other reasons, we
are now forming a new federal committee to be administered in
California, which committee will be entirely separate from the
state CREPAC. We will hereafter file our own federal reports and
transmit our net proceeds to RPAC as our "affiliated committee."

     If we can provide any further information, please do not
hesitate to ask, and if you want us to meet with you at some
convenient time, we would be pleased to do so.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.
VGN:jy
encs.
(dictated but not read)
cc:  Greg Fletcher
     Ted Prim, FPPC

August 26, 1977


Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.
Dobbs & Nielsen
Alcoa Building
1 Maritime Plaza
Suite 2500
San Francisco, California 94111

Dear Chip:

On May 6, 1977, Franchise Tax Board submitted to the Fair
Political Practices Commission and the Attorney General its
audit report of California Real Estate Political Action Committee
(CREPAC) covering the period January 7, 1975 through January 29,
1976. This letter will be limited to one of the findings in
that audit report.

That finding is as follows:

UNREGISTERED COMMITTEES

CREPAC Trustees set a yearly contribution goal
for each local BORPAC. Funds transmitted by the
BORPACs are first applied towards their indivi-
dual preset goals with the excess funds, if any,
earmarked and held in trust for the local BORPAC.

The local BORPAC may disburse their earmarked
excess funds as they choose with the approval of
a CREPAC Trustee(s). It was determined that
many BORPACs with earmarked funds in excess of
$500.00 are committees as defined in Government
Code Section 82013(a) and have not registered.

Reference Exhibit A [which listed the
names of 48 local BORPACs.]

Mr. O'Donnel stated that the campaign statements
filed by CREPAC covered the activities of CREPAC
and the BORPACs.

Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.                                    August 26, 1977
Page Two


         The staff of the Fair Political Practices Commission has
reviewed the factual circumstances surrounding the CREPAC/BORPAC
contributions. We have also reviewed all audit correspondence
between you and the Franchise Tax Board and have concluded that
CREPAC is a committee and is also an intermediary for each of the
BORPACs which qualify as committees. We agree with your stipulated
facts contained in the second paragraph on page two of your July
21, 1977, letter.

         We agree with the Franchise Tax Board's conclusion that a
local BORPAC becomes a committee within the meaning of the Politi-
cal Reform Act when CREPAC credits $500 or more to the BORPAC's
account. Once CREPAC meets its goal with respect to any given
BORPAC, control over all additional funds is transferred to the
BORPAC. Thus, even though CREPAC retains a right of approval
over all contributions, the real authority to determine the size
and recipient of contributions is transferred to the BORPAC.
CREPAC merely acts as the agent of BORPAC by holding earmarked
funds in its account until it receives instructions from the
BORPAC. We think that funds which have been earmarked for an
individual BORPAC and whose disposition is controlled by the
BORPAC have been "recalled" by the BORPAC within the meaning of
the Political Reform Act. Accordingly, once the amount of such
funds reaches $500 or more, the BORPAC will be a committee within
the meaning of Government Code Section 82013. Lastly, since
all contributions ultimately received by an individual BORPAC
pass through a CREPAC account, CREPAC is an intermediary within
the meaning of Government Code Section 24302. However, since
no contributor for which CREPAC acts as an intermediary makes
contributions of $50 or more, CREPAC need not be identified as
an intermediary on a local BORPAC's campaign statement.

         In summary, individual BORPACs which qualify as committees
within the Political Reform Act must file individual campaign
statements.

         I hope this will allow you to communicate with the local
BORPACs which have qualified as committees to advise them how
to file. If you have any questions, please feel free to
contact me.

                                                    Sincerely,

                                                    /s/
                                                    Riad Prim
                                                    Staff Counsel
                                                    Legal Division

CREPAC                                    CALIFORNIA REAL ESTATE
                                                   POLITICAL ACTION COMMITTEE
                                                   Executive Offices: 505 SHATTO PLACE
                                                   LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90020
                                                   TELEPHONE AREA CODE 213 - 380-7190

                                                   Campaign Identification No. 742276


November 10, 1977


Ted Prim, Staff Counsel
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Prim:

This is in response to your letter of September 23, 1977, to our legal
counsel, Vigo C. Nielsen, Jr., advising us on alternatives that
fulfill the filing requirements under the Political Reform Act of 1974.
The Board of CREPAC Trustees has met and has instructed me to write
you to ensure that we are proceeding in the correct manner.

Enclosed please find a copy of an amendment to the CREPAC Statement
of Organization to clarify the identity of contributions made in the
name of CREPAC/BORPAC. As you will notice, the registration
provides a complete listing of the names and addresses of each BORPAC.
This amendment will be filed with the Secretary of State and will be
transmitted to each filing official to whom BORPAC contributions were
made between 1975 and August 1977 with the accompanying transmittal
letter of explanation.

We have decided to select your alternative Number three as the appropriate
disclosure of all BORPAC contributions. We are requiring that each BORPAC
include instructions to the donee in its transmittal letters, but to ensure
compliance, we are reprinting the BORPAC checks to read as follows:
"CREPAC (_________________ BORPAC), Board of Realtors© Political Action
Committee, (address), I.D. No. 742276." The name of the board and the
address will be typed individually on each check.

We are also changing the format for our campaign statements. The "name
of committee" shall read "California Real Estate Political Action Committee
(CREPAC), also known as (name of board) BORPAC – see names of BORPACs

Ted Prim, Staff Counsel
November 10, 1977
Page two


on CREPAC Statement of Organization.") Also, the contributions made
will be arranged on the report under the headings for CREPAC and each
participating BORPAC, and cumulations will be noted by astericks when
CREPAC/ and/or one or more BORPAC contributes to the same candidate.
Further, as requested in your letter, and even though we do not believe
such is required by the Act, we will provide an attachment to each report
identifying total contributions by the BORPAC region which the contribution
income is received.

We trust that this fully complies with the intent, spirit and letter of your
requirements, and that this brings us into total compliance with the Act.
If you have any questions or reservations about the actions that we
plan to undertake within the next few days, please contact me immediately.

At the request of John Gordnier, Deputy Attorney General, I am also
sending him a copy of the letter. I am also sending a copy to the Marin
County District Attorney.

Very truly yours,

Greg Fletcher
Greg Fletcher, Treasurer
CREPAC

enclosures

cc:    John A. Gordnier, Deputy Attorney General
       Bruce Bales, Marin County District Attorney (Attention: Joshua
       Thomas, Assistant District Attorney)
       Fred Stowell, CREPAC Chairman
       Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr., Esq.
       Dana W. Reed, Esq.

CALIFORNIA REAL ESTATE
POLITICAL ACTION COMMITTEE
Executive Offices: 505 SHATTO PLACE
LOS ANGELES CALIFORNIA 90020
TELEPHONE AREA CODE 213 - 380-7190

Campaign Identification No. 742276

November 4, 1977


TO:     Secretary of State
        County Clerks
        City Clerks
        Filing Officials of Campaign Statements

FROM:   California Real Estate Political Action Committee (CREPAC
        also known as" [ Name of Board ] Board of Realtors® Political
        Action Committee" BORPAC.

Dear Filing Official:

CREPAC is a single committee and has been in operation since 1969. We
have been filing campaign statements with all filing officials for each
state and local election to whom we have made contributions. We have
an internal mechanism that allows Realtors® in most counties to decide
how some of the funds shall be spent. These funds are identified as
BORPAC contributions.

Campaigns receiving funds from us have been reporting them as being received
from CREPAC I.D.#742276, from BORPAC I.D.#742276 or from name of
board BORPAC I.D.#742276. Upon the recent advice of the Fair Political
Practices Commission, recipients in the future will be told exactly how to
report the contribution, and the checks are being reprinted to provide
a combined name of CREPAC/_______________BORPAC.

The F.P.P.C. has also asked that we write you to explain that any contributions
reported from CREPAC during these last four years could have been reported
by the recipient in any of the three manners set forth above. Attached is a
copy of our recent amendment to our Statement of Organization to better
identify each BORPAC by name and address. Please retain this copy in your
files as our clarification for all contributions previously reported in your
jurisdiction.

Thank you.


cc: Fair Political Practices Commission ___
    Attorney General's Office

BOARD OF REALTORS
POLITICAL ACTION
COMMITTEE

Campaign Identification No. 742276

Date


Dear Recipient:

Attached please find a check from your local Board of Realtors®
Political Action Committee_________________________(BORPAC).
We report all BORPAC contributions in your county to the
Secretary of State and the local filing officials. Please refer
to the enclosed check for proper name, address and I.D.
number to be used on your campaign statement.

Good luck in your campaign.

Sincerely,


Fred Stowell, Chairman
CREPAC Trustees

Enclosure




Administrative Offices: 505 SHATTO PLACE - LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90020 - Telephone (213) 380-7190

DOBBS & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law

SUITE 2500, THE ALCOA BUILDING
ONE MARITIME PLAZA
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94111

TELEPHONE (415) 362-1940

February 1, 1978


Ted Prim, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: CREPAC

Dear Ted:

     This is to confirm our telephone conversation
that the proposed steps to be taken by CREPAC, as set
forth in my letter of January 20, 1978, will bring
this committee into full compliance with the law.

     Thank you for your assistance in this matter.

                                   Very truly yours,

                                   Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.

VGN:jy
cc: CREPAC
     Marin County District Attorney, Attn. Josh Thomas
     John A. Gordnier, Esq., Deputy Attorney General


SACRAMENTO OFFICE • SUITE 544, 1127 ELEVENTH STREET, SACRAMENTO, CA 95814 • (916) 446-6752

DOBBS & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law
SUITE 2500, THE ALCOA BUILDING
ONE MARITIME PLAZA
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94111

TELEPHONE (415) 362-1940

January 20, 1978


Ted Prim, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Ted:

My apologies for being so late in replying to
your telephone call requesting few additional changes to
bring CREPAC/BORPAC into compliance with your original
letter of September 23, 1977.

Since some time has elapsed, let me summarize
my memory of the facts to ensure that I have not missed
anything which might further delay final resolution of
this matter.

I understand the conclusions of your September 23,
1977 letter to be as follows:

1. Contributions made by California Realtors
can either be filed on a consolidated CREPAC/BORPAC campaign
statement or CREPAC can report all contributions to State
candidates made by its trustees, and all contributions made
on the recommendations of local Realtors (and debited against
their "BORPAC account") can be filed separately by each BORPAC.
CREPAC has decided to file a consolidated report.

2. Since a consolidated report will be filed,
contributions must all be made in the name of CREPAC to
allow easy verification of such contributions on either
the donor or the donee campaign statement. This will be
accomplished by (a) revising the transmittal letter of "BORPAC"
to indicate that such terminology is only used informally to
explain the recommendation procedures of the Realtors in a
local area who approved the contributions; (b) by modifying
the "BORPAC" check to indicate that the committee is CREPAC
and that the recommendation for the contribution has been
made by local Realtors; (c) that it is acceptable to further
clarify the CREPAC contribution by a parenthetical designation
describing the area from which the local Realtors reside who
made the recommendation (for example, CREPAC (Sacramento BORPAC)).


SACRAMENTO OFFICE · SUITE 544, 1127 ELEVENTH STREET, SACRAMENTO, CA 95814 · (916) 446-6752

Ted Prim
January 20, 1978
Page Two


     3.  That of your five suggested procedures for
describing the contribution, CREPAC has selected alternative
"3" which will designate all contributions recommended by the
political active Realtors in Sacramento as being a contribu-
tion from "CREPAC (Sacramento BORPAC)".

     4.  The combined CREPAC report will voluntarily
disclose the aggregate of contributions received from each
local BORPAC district and will disclose expenditures made
according to local BORPAC districts that authorize them.

     5.  To assist the filing officials and the public
who presently may be unable to verify donee contributions
with the donor report, we will file with each filing official
in the State where CREPAC/BORPAC contributions have been
made since the inception of the Political Reform Act, a
clarifying memo and statement of cross-reference (both
attached) which the filing official can file under "BORPAC"
to inform the public that the donor committee can be found
under "CREPAC."

     I hope this summary is correct. On November 10,
1977, Greg Fletcher, CREPAC Treasurer, wrote you, and sent
copies to the Attorney General's office and the Marin County
District Attorney's office, advising you that the CREPAC
Statement of Organization has been amended to inform the
Secretary of State that contributions would be made under the
name of CREPAC/BORPAC and a list of the complete name and
address of each "BORPAC" was made part of this amendment.
The letter further describes that the checks would be
reprinted to read "CREPAC (name of Board) BORPAC, Board of
Realtors Political Action Committee, (address), I.D. No.
742276." After my conversation with you, we have agreed to
delete the phrase "Board of Realtors Political Action
Committee" and will instead have the check read "California
Real Estate Political Action Committee ((name of Board) BORPAC),
(address), I.D. No. 742276." Mr. Fletcher's letter also
explained that the Statement of Organization and all subse-
quent campaign reports would be identified as "California
Real Estate Political Action Committee (CREPAC), also known as
CREPAC/________* BORPAC-* see name of BORPACs on CREPAC
Statement of Organization." CREPAC plans to continue to
identify its report in this manner.

     Mr. Fletcher also indicated that all cumulative
totals will be noted for contributions to the same campaigns,
even if the recommendations for the contributions come from

Ted Prim
January 20, 1978
Page Three


different BORPACs. And he indicated that, "even though we do
not believe such is required by the Act, we will provide an
attachment to each report identifying total contributions by
the BORPAC region (from) which the contribution income is
received." This is now being done on each report.

Apparently, Mr. Fletcher's office inadvertently
omitted from the letter sent you the amendment to the State-
ment of Organization, the proposed transmittal letter to the
filing officials and the proposed revised transmittal letter
by which recipients will receive contribution checks. These
were provided on December 8, 1977, and the subject of our
last telephone conversation.

Enclosed please find a revision of our transmittal
letter to filing officials, a proposed "cross-reference"
document to accompany that transmittal to allow filing
officials to place such sheet in the "BORPAC" file, and
a proposed revision to the transmittal letter to candi-
dates of contributions made on the request of a BORPAC.
When you have a chance to review these documents, I would
appreciate being able to discuss it with you and hopefully
resolving this matter once and for all.

Very truly yours,

Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr.

VGN:bc

Encl.

cc: CREPAC
    Marin County District Attorney, Attn. Josh Thomas
    John A. Gordnier, Deputy Attorney General

(On CREPAC Stationery)

FEB 3 [illegible] 47 AM '78

January    , 1978


To:  Secretary of State
     County Clerks
     City Clerks, as
     Filing Officials of Campaign Statements

Fr:  California Real Estate Political Action Committee


Dear Filing Official:

CREPAC is a single committee and has been in operation

since 1969. We have been filing campaign statements

with all filing officials for each state and local

election to whom we have made contributions. We have

an internal mechanism that allows Realtors[circled R] in most

counties to decide how some of the funds shall be spent.

The Realtors who comprise this advisory committee are

identified as, for example in Sacramento County, the

Sacramento Board of Realtors Political Action Committee

or Sacramento BORPAC. The funds contributed are identified

as BORPAC contributions.

Apparently, in the past campaigns receiving funds from us

have been reporting them as being received from 1) CREPAC

I.D. #742276, from 2) BORPAC I.D. #742276 or from 3) "name

of board" BORPAC I.D. #742276. Upon the recent advice of

the Fair Political Practices Commission, recipients at

present and in the future will be told to report the

contribution, as being made by California Real Estate

Political Action Committee[(name of board) BORPAC], I.D.

742276, or CREPAC[(name of board) BORPAC], I.D. 742276.

The FPPC has also asked that we write you to explain

than any contributions reported from CREPAC during these

last four years could have been reported by the recipient

in any of the three manners set forth above. Attached is

a copy of our recent amendment to our Statement of

Organization to better identify each "BORPAC" by name

and address. Please retain this copy in your files as

our clarification for all contributions previously reported

in your jurisdiction. Also attached is a simple "cross

reference letter" required of us by the FPPC, which they

suggest you place in any file in your office marked

BORPAC or (name of board) BORPAC to allow the public to

know that they should inspect our CREPAC report filed

with you for such contributions made by us.

Thank you.


cc: Fair Political Practices Commission
    Attorney General's Office

(On CREPAC stationery)

(Date)

Re: ____________* BORPAC contributions
                and
          BORPAC contributions


NOTE: Contributions reported by donee campaign committees
under either of the two names above can be found on the
California Real Estate Political Action Committee (CREPAC)
campaign statement, I.D. No. 742276, which committee was
the donor of such contribution(s).


EXPLANATION: The California Real Estate Political Action
Committee (CREPAC) is the statewide political action
committee for voluntary contributions from participating
Realtors®. In the past when a recommendation for con-
tributions was made by Realtors in your area, the
contribution was identified as "(name of Board) Board of
Realtors Political Action Committee, I.D. No. 742276" or
"BORPAC, I.D. No. 742276."

     At the present, and in the future, all donees
of contributions will be required to disclose the con-
tribution as being received from "California Real Estate
Political Action Committee ((name of Board) BORPAC), I.D.
No. 742276" or "CREPAC((name of Board) BORPAC), I.D. No.
742276."

     Thank you.


*we will insert the actual name of the BORPAC
 in that area.

CREPAC/BORPAC                                    I.D. No. 742276


Date

Dear
                    Attached please find a check from California

Real Estate Political Action Committee (____________**

BORPAC).  The term BORPAC is our identification of

contributions from local Realtors® within your county,

and this contributions has been made at their request.

This contribution should be disclosed as being received

from CREPAC (____________** BORPAC), ________** (address)

________________, identification number 742276.

                    Good luck in your campaign.

Sincerely,


Your local BORPAC

Enclosure


* new logo that does not identify as BORPAC
** we will type in individual name and address




Administrative Offices: 505 SHATTO PLACE, LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90020

embedding.qa_text: **State of California Fair Political Practices Commission** P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance/Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest (916) 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 September 23, 1977 Vigo G. Nielsen, Jr. Dobbs and Nielsen One Maritime Plaza, Suite 2500 San Francisco, CA 94111 Dear Mr. Nielsen: After receiving my letter of August 26, 1977, supporting the Franchise Tax Board's audit finding on unregistered committees, you once again expressed your desire to bring CREPAC and all local BORPACs into compliance with the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act. Accordingly, you ask two questions. First, you ask whether there were any possible way for CREPAC and local BORPACs to file a statement as one committee. In terms of the language of the Act, in other words, could CREPAC and its local BORPACs be viewed as a combination of persons or as persons acting in concert? Second, what action should be taken by CREPAC and its local BORPACs relative to the amendment of campaign statements previously filed with local jurisdictions throughout the state? In addition to the information contained in my letter of August 26, the following facts are material to the conclusions set forth in this letter. In recent telephone conversations you informed me that local BORPACs are equivalent to local subcommittees of CREPAC. Thus, the finances and operations of each local BORPAC must be handled in accordance with CREPAC bylaws. These bylaws can be changed by the CREPAC directors without the consent of the local BORPACs. Moreover, the power to write all BORPAC checks and the technical authority to disapprove a contribution requested by a BORPAC is retained by an officer of CREPAC. Given the facts set forth above, we think it would be permissible for CREPAC and its local BORPACs to file campaign statements in the manner set forth in the FTB audit report or as a single committee. If, however, you should choose to file as a single committee, your present practice of filing campaign statements under the name "CREPAC" while recipients of your contributions simultaneously disclose the contributor as a local BORPAC must be discontinued. In order to comply with the provisions of the Political Reform Act, you must take reasonable steps to assure that your committee will be identified by the same name on its committee statement and on the campaign statements of any person contributing to or receiving funds from your committee. Perhaps the transmittal letter included with each contribution would be the appropriate vehicle for accomplishing this task. In addition to listing the name of the committee, it is permissible to ask contribution recipients to include a reference to a local BORPAC in their campaign statements. For example, your transmittal letter might suggest that the contribution from CREPAC be disclosed in any of the following ways: 1. CREPAC 2. CREPAC and 49 BORPACs 3. CREPAC (Sacramento BORPAC) 4. CREPAC through its Sacramento BORPAC 5.
